file_name: Al-Yousef et al. - 2025 - Enhancing melanoma detection through multiple datasets integration and robust deep learning.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically ResNet-50 and MobileNetV2, to perform binary and multiclass classification of melanoma and other skin lesions using datasets such as Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset, HAM10000, and ISIC2018.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the integration of multiple datasets (Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset, HAM10000, ISIC2018) and the development/validation of deep learning models (ResNet-50, MobileNetV2) for melanoma detection. It includes specific methodology, results (e.g., 99.8% accuracy), and statistical analysis, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enhancing melanoma detection through multiple datasets integration and robust deep learning
brief_summary: This study aimed to enhance melanoma detection accuracy by integrating multiple datasets and evaluating the robustness of deep learning models. The researchers utilized the Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset and HAM10000 for training binary and multiclass classifiers, specifically ResNet-50 and MobileNetV2. Model performance was assessed using an independent test set from ISIC2018 to evaluate generalization and robustness across different data distributions. The results indicated that ResNet-50 achieved the highest classification accuracy of 99.8% when trained on the integrated dataset, outperforming MobileNetV2. However, robustness tests revealed that binary models trained on single datasets performed poorly (50% accuracy) on unseen data, whereas models trained on pooled datasets maintained higher accuracy (85-98.8%). The study concludes that integrating multiple datasets significantly improves model robustness and detection reliability for melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for binary and multiclass classification. Used in Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset (3140 samples), HAM10000 (1113 samples), and ISIC2018 (500 samples).
Included in multiclass classification task. Used in Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset (3323 samples) and HAM10000 (327 samples).
Included in multiclass classification task. Used in Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset (4500 samples) and HAM10000 (1500 samples).
Included in multiclass classification task. Used in Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset (2079 samples).

paper_terms: Seborrheic Keratosis and other Benign Tumours
lesion_types_studied.details: Included in multiclass classification task. Used in Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset (1847 samples).
Used as the negative class in binary classification (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma). Includes BCC, SCC, and other non-melanoma types mentioned in introduction.
Used as the negative class in binary classification. Includes BKL, SK, and other benign tumors from the Kaggle dataset.

Mentioned in the introduction as a common non-melanoma skin cancer (23% of cases) and in the Kaggle dataset description as 'Seborrheic Keratosis and other Benign Tumours' (note: paper uses SK for Seborrheic Keratosis in the multiclass section, but SCC is listed in the dataset description text). The multiclass experiment specifically lists 'Seborrheic Keratosis and other Benign Tumours' as the class, distinct from SCC mentioned in the intro.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma/Benign) and Multiclass classification (BCC, NV, BKL, SK, Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the use of 'binary classification' to determine if a lesion is cancerous or benign, and 'multiclass classification' to identify several categories of skin illnesses. Models (ResNet-50, MobileNetV2) were trained to classify images into discrete diagnostic categories. Performance was measured using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset (KSD)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from Kaggle containing images of various skin disorders. Used for training binary and multiclass classifiers.
classes_used: Seborrheic Keratosis and other Benign Tumours (SK)

use.training: Used for training binary and multiclass classifiers (ResNet-50 and MobileNetV2). Split into 80% training and 20% validation.
use.validation: Used as the validation set (20% of the dataset).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of dermoscopic images of skin lesions. Used to augment the training data to improve model robustness and generalization.

use.training: Pooled with the Kaggle dataset to train the classifiers for the second part of the study.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2018
datasets_used.description: An independent test dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration. Used to evaluate the robustness and generalization of the trained models on unseen data.

use.testing: Used as an independent test set to verify model robustness and generalization.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the performance of binary and multiclass classifiers (ResNet-50, MobileNetV2) by calculating accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV.
To assess model robustness and generalization by testing models trained on Kaggle/HAM10000 datasets against the unseen ISIC2018 dataset.
To compare the performance of models trained on single datasets versus pooled (integrated) datasets to determine the impact of data integration on accuracy and sensitivity.
To report the performance metrics (Accuracy, Recall, Specificity, Precision, NPV, F1Score) for models trained on multiple datasets, indicating variability in results.

statistical_methods_used.method: Image Hashing and Cosine Similarity Check
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure no data leakage or overlap between training/validation splits and the independent test set (ISIC2018) by verifying patient-level separation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests, ANOVA) for statistical significance. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) and confusion matrices.

Kaggle_Skin_Disease_Dataset_Part1.notes: Binary classifiers outperformed multiclass classifiers. ResNet-50 achieved 99.8% training accuracy.

Kaggle_Skin_Disease_Dataset_Part1_Multic.notes: ResNet-50 outperformed MobileNetV2 in both training and testing phases. Binary classification was superior to multiclass classification.

ISIC2018_Robustness_Test_Single_Dataset.experiment: Robustness Testing (Models trained on Kaggle only, tested on ISIC2018)

ResNet-50.Notes: Poor performance due to domain shift; samples from different datasets collected under different conditions.

ISIC2018_Robustness_Test_Single_Dataset.notes: Binary models trained on single datasets performed poorly on unseen data.

Integrated_Dataset_Kaggle_HAM10000.notes: ResNet-50 achieved 98.54% testing accuracy. Performance improved over single-dataset training (e.g., ResNet-50 sensitivity increased by 7.8%).

ISIC2018_Robustness_Test_Integrated_Data.experiment: Robustness Testing (Models trained on Kaggle+HAM10000, tested on ISIC2018)

ResNet-50.Notes: Maintained higher accuracy compared to single-dataset models (50%). Abstract mentions 98.8% success rate for pooled models generally, but specific robustness test result for ResNet-50 is 85%.

MobileNetV2.Notes: Abstract states pooled models achieved 98.8% success rate, but specific ISIC2018 robustness test details for MobileNetV2 are not explicitly separated from the general pooled statement in the abstract. Table 6 lists MobileNetV2 accuracy as 88.7% on Kaggle+HAM10000 test set, but does not explicitly list the ISIC2018 robustness accuracy for MobileNetV2 separately from the general pooled statement.

ISIC2018_Robustness_Test_Integrated_Data.notes: Models trained on pooled datasets maintained higher accuracy on unseen data compared to single-dataset models.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported in the provided context (standard ImageNet pretraining is implied by the model names but not stated).

ResNet-50.layers: 50 layers total (48 convolutional layers, 1 MaxPool layer, 1 intermediate pooling layer)
ResNet-50.components: Convolutional layers with set filters, Deep Residual Features (DRF) from the last convolutional layer, Fully Connected (FC) layer with 1,000 neurons (standard architecture description referenced)

MobileNetV2.type: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for mobile devices
MobileNetV2.components: Depthwise Separable Convolutions (DSC) consisting of Depthwise Convolution (DC) and Pointwise Convolution (PC), Batch Normalization, ReLU activation, MaxPooling layer, Fully Connected layers, SoftMax output layer

training_details.learning_rate: Small learning rate (specific value not reported)

preprocessing_techniques: Pixel Standardization (normalization) using the formula Ni = (Xi - µ) / σ, where Xi is the original pixel value, µ is the mean, and σ is the standard deviation
Use of a small learning rate to minimize vanishing gradient

Data leakage prevention via patient-level separation and image hashing with cosine similarity check on CNN embeddings to ensure no overlap between training/validation splits and the independent test set (ISIC2018)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not report specific geometric or color transform augmentations (e.g., rotation, flipping, brightness adjustment). Instead, it utilized data integration as a method to enhance robustness and generalization. The Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset (KSD) and HAM10000 were pooled together to create a larger, more heterogeneous training set. The authors noted that 'intra-class variability increased dramatically after merging the two datasets,' which 'clearly enhanced the model’s capacity for generalization' and acted as a form of augmentation by introducing domain diversity (variation in light levels, color tones, and magnification) rather than synthetic image transformations.

conclusions: Binary classifiers outperformed multiclass classifiers in melanoma detection, with ResNet-50 achieving a training accuracy of 99.8% and a testing accuracy of 97.48% on the Kaggle dataset.
Models trained on a single dataset (Kaggle) exhibited poor robustness when tested on the independent ISIC2018 dataset, achieving only 50% accuracy.

Integrating multiple datasets (Kaggle Skin Disease Dataset and HAM10000) significantly improved model robustness and generalization, with pooled models achieving a classification success rate of 98.8%.
ResNet-50 trained on the integrated dataset maintained higher robustness on the unseen ISIC2018 test set, achieving 85% accuracy compared to the 50% accuracy of single-dataset models.
The integration of datasets increased intra-class variability, which enhanced the model's capacity for generalization and reduced class imbalance.
ResNet-50 consistently outperformed MobileNetV2 in both binary and multiclass classification tasks across internal and external test sets.

The study highlights that using a single dataset for both training and testing can lead to poor performance on unseen data, emphasizing the need for external validation to assess real-world clinical utility.

Future work will address limitations regarding imaging domain similarity by incorporating non-dermatoscopic data (e.g., smartphone camera images) and addressing demographic bias through domain adaptation and fairness-aware reweighting.
important_remarks: Binary classification (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma) significantly outperformed multiclass classification in terms of accuracy and robustness.

Models trained on a single dataset (Kaggle) exhibited severe domain bias, achieving only 50% accuracy on the independent ISIC2018 test set, highlighting the risk of overfitting to specific imaging domains.
Dataset integration (Kaggle + HAM10000) substantially improved model robustness, with ResNet-50 achieving 85% accuracy on the external ISIC2018 test set compared to 50% for single-dataset models.

The study acknowledges that all training datasets were derived from clinical dermoscopic imaging, limiting real-world applicability; future work plans to incorporate non-dermatoscopic data (e.g., smartphone images).
The authors note potential demographic bias in the datasets and propose future domain adaptation and fairness-aware reweighting to address this limitation.

MobileNetV2 performance degraded when trained on the integrated dataset compared to single-dataset training, whereas ResNet-50 maintained high performance, suggesting architecture-specific sensitivity to data heterogeneity.

The study utilized image hashing and cosine similarity checks to prevent data leakage between the training/validation splits and the independent test set (ISIC2018), ensuring patient-level separation.
